topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door made my heart jump into my throat. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the deadbolt. It was a package, taped shut with rough brown paper, addressed to the old Mrs. Henderson, the tenant who'd moved out last spring. A knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, unpleasant squeeze. I took a deep breath, the air tasting stale in the hallway. When I finally opened the door, the sight of the box made my fingers tremble slightly. It felt like an intrusion, a reminder of a life I wasn't part of, a history I didn't share. I took the package inside, setting it on the worn kitchen table, and stared at it, unable to move. Every small sound in the apartment seemed amplified—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—and I felt an overwhelming urge to just close the door and disappear.

[story 2]

I found the box tucked behind the radiator. It was heavy, wrapped in brittle, yellowed paper. My palms grew slick as I unwrapped the twine, the crinkling sound loud in the quiet flat. Inside, nestled in packing peanuts, was a collection of old photographs. As I lifted the first one, a faint tremor ran up my arm. It wasn't the contents that unsettled me; it was the implication—someone else’s narrative suddenly bleeding into my space. I sat on the edge of the sofa, clutching the photos so tightly my knuckles turned white. My breathing became shallow, quick little catches in my chest. I kept glancing at the front door, half-expecting someone to walk in, to acknowledge the unexpected weight of this forgotten history.

[story 3]

The delivery notice had been vague, just a note saying 'previous resident.' I felt a strange tightness spreading through my shoulders as I approached the mail slot. When I pulled the parcel out, the cardboard felt strangely cold against my fingertips. I carried it to my room, the silence of the apartment suddenly pressing in on me. Opening it was an act of pure hesitation. Inside was a small, intricately carved wooden bird. Looking at it, I felt a sudden, sharp awareness of my own isolation. My gaze darted around the room, cataloging every shadow, every corner, as if searching for an explanation for why this object, belonging to someone else, had landed in my hands. A slow, shaky exhale escaped me, and I sank onto the floor, the weight of the unknown pressing down.
